## 2026-03-06 Update

Resolution events:
- Southern Lebanon district resolved to Tyre district. I had Tyre as a tail but not first. Lesson: for southern Lebanon building/strike district markets, include "Tyre district" exactly, not only the city name "Tyre".
- Nepal PM resolved to Balendra Shah. The strong March 5 move was correct.
- School-accusation country resolved to Iran. The March 5 conditional shift after US-military responsibility was correct.

Updates made:
- Oslo embassy markets: no direct Oslo reporting, but March 6 JPost/NSC reporting warned of concrete attack concerns for Israelis abroad and synagogue/Israeli-target incidents. Raised Israeli Embassy/Israel modestly.
- Beirut coastal hotel district: no exact hotel-room report yet; raised Raouche as the likeliest coastal hotel district while keeping Manara/Ain el-Mreisseh/Ramlet al-Baida/Minet el-Hosn live.

Watched but no update:
- Beirut apartment district, Sri Lanka cricket coach, Australia missile recipient, airline outlook, Ukraine counteroffensive region, Iran World Cup venue country, Iraqi militia casualties, China AFC quarterfinal opponent, Liverpool UCL scorer, IPL opener, PSG-Chelsea substitute scorer, medical-device cyberattack persona, Real Madrid-Man City hat-trick, central Beirut apartment organisation, Detroit synagogue, and western Iraq aircraft model had no March 6 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-07 Update

Resolution events:
- Beirut coastal hotel-room district resolved to Raouche. The March 6 Raouche shift was correct. Lesson: coastal Beirut hotel/apartment strike markets can resolve to Raouche, not only southern-suburb labels.
- Oslo embassy target resolved to US embassy / United States. I missed by overweighting Israeli-target warnings. Lesson: for exact embassy questions, keep US Embassy/United States materially live even when Israel-target risk dominates adjacent coverage.
- Beirut apartment district also resolved to Raouche. I missed by keeping the apartment market in Dahiyeh/Haret Hreik/Ghobeiry. Lesson: "central/coastal Beirut" and apartment wording can point to Raouche; do not map all Beirut apartment strikes to Dahiyeh.

Updates made:
- KC-135/base country: Straits Times/HT reported Saudi Arabia intercepted a ballistic missile launched toward Prince Sultan Air Base near Riyadh, which houses US military personnel. Raised Saudi Arabia strongly for the KC-135 damaged-base country.
- Iranian island: Al Jazeera reported Iranian officials saying a US attack hit a freshwater desalination plant on Qeshm Island. Shifted the Trump/island forecast to Qeshm Island while preserving Qeshm/Kharg exact-string variants.
- Launch-area country: Al Jazeera quoted Iran's FM tying de-escalation to neighbours' airspace/territory/waters not being used, and Iranian officials referred to an airbase in a southern neighbouring country after the Qeshm attack. Added UAE and Bahrain while cutting Israel/Jordan/Iraq.
- Iraqi militia casualties: March 7 Al Jazeera reported PMF/Hashd al-Shaabi bases in Nineveh were hit and a PMF fighter was killed after a likely US air strike near Mosul. Added Popular Mobilisation Forces while keeping Kataib Hezbollah first because earlier Al Jazeera/Reuter-style reporting named Kataib Hezbollah in Iraqi strike casualties.
- Gulf water/power plant and Abu Dhabi company markets: UAE was described as the most heavily targeted Gulf state, with al-Dhafra, Abu Dhabi airport, Dubai airport, the US consulate in Dubai, and other sites hit. Raised UAE in the water/power plant country and raised ADNOC for the Abu Dhabi company-site damage market.
- AFC Women semifinal: Market background now states Australia and South Korea are set to play the other semifinal. Raised Australia as the team most likely to beat South Korea.

Watched but no update:
- Sri Lanka men's cricket coach, Australia missile recipient, airline financial outlook, Ukraine counteroffensive region, Iran World Cup venue country, China quarterfinal opponent, Liverpool UCL scorer, IPL opener, PSG-Chelsea substitute scorer, medical-device cyberattack persona, Real Madrid-Man City hat-trick, central Beirut apartment organisation, Detroit synagogue, Michigan synagogue assailant, western Iraq aircraft model, Virginia terror-shooting university, Hormuz nonparticipation country, and Trump 15-point-plan intermediary had no March 7 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-08 Update

Resolution events:
- Sri Lanka men's cricket coach resolved to Gary Kirsten. I missed by over-weighting Sri Lankan former players. Lesson: cricket-coach markets can name major foreign coaches when boards seek quick outside credibility.
- Australia's missile-recipient country resolved to United Arab Emirates. I missed badly by anchoring on Ukraine. Lesson: in the simulated Gulf war, Australian missile assistance can go to Gulf partners/UAE, not just Ukraine.
- Airline outlook suspension resolved to Air New Zealand. I missed by focusing on Qantas and larger European/US carriers. Lesson: include Air New Zealand in fuel-cost/outlook markets tied to regional aviation shocks.
- Ukrainian region almost entirely retaken resolved to Dnipropetrovsk. I missed by omitting Dnipropetrovsk from counteroffensive-region tails. Lesson: include Dnipropetrovsk oblast alongside Kherson, Zaporizhzhia, Sumy, Donetsk, and Kharkiv.

Updates made:
- China AFC Women's Asian Cup quarterfinal opponent: Straits Times and Al Jazeera reported that Australia will travel to Perth to play either North Korea or reigning champions China in the last eight. Raised Australia strongly.
- Iraqi militia casualties: March 8 Straits Times described Hashed/PMF bases hosting Iran-aligned groups, "mostly Kataeb Hezbollah", and listed Kataeb Hezbollah, al-Nujaba, Kataeb Sayyid al-Shuhada, and Kataeb Imam Ali. Shifted weight toward the Kataeb spelling while keeping Kataib Hezbollah and PMF.
- KC-135/base country: Straits Times said Saudi Arabia destroyed three ballistic missiles heading toward Prince Sultan Air Base, which hosts American troops. Raised Saudi Arabia further.
- Gulf water/power plant country: Bahrain reported material damage to a desalination plant from an Iranian drone attack. Added Bahrain as the lead while preserving Qatar/Kuwait/UAE as infrastructure-risk tails.
- Central Beirut apartment organisation: multiple March 8 reports said the Beirut hotel/apartment strike targeted commanders from Iran's Quds Force/IRGC rather than Hezbollah. Shifted from Hezbollah to Quds Force/IRGC variants.
- Launch-area country: Iranian/IRGC claims and March 8 reports specifically alleged al-Dhafra air base in the UAE was used as a launchpad. Raised UAE and kept Saudi/Bahrain/Qatar as regional airbase tails.
- Western Iraq aircraft model: Al Jazeera/NDTV described an unexploded US kamikaze drone in western Iraq that looked like LUCAS and noted CENTCOM's combat debut of one-way attack drones. Added LUCAS and exact-system variants above MQ-9 Reaper.
- Hormuz nonparticipation country: no exact "Strait of Hormuz" refusal article yet, but Spain has repeatedly refused US use of Rota/Moron for Iran attacks and framed its position as "no to war". Raised Spain over Germany with small European tails.

Watched but no update:
- Iran World Cup venue country, Liverpool UCL scorer, IPL opener, PSG-Chelsea substitute scorer, medical-device cyberattack persona, Real Madrid-Man City hat-trick, Detroit synagogue, Michigan synagogue assailant, Virginia terror-shooting university, Lebanon health-centre village, Abu Dhabi company-site damage, and Trump 15-point-plan intermediary had no March 8 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-09 Update

Resolution events:
- Iran World Cup group-stage move resolved to Mexico. Canada was first, but Mexico was close. Lesson: when US-hosted matches become politically impossible, Mexico is likelier than Canada for football/soccer logistics with Latin American fan access and Monterrey-style venues.
- Iraqi militia casualties resolved to Kataib Imam Ali. I missed by focusing on "mostly Kataeb Hezbollah" instead of preserving the full list of named Iraqi factions. Lesson: when an article lists Kataeb Hezbollah, al-Nujaba, Kataeb Sayyid al-Shuhada, and Kataeb Imam Ali, reserve material probability for each exact faction.
- China's AFC Women's Asian Cup quarterfinal opponent resolved to Taiwan. I missed badly by reading "China will play Group C second" as Australia/North Korea uncertainty. Lesson: include Taiwan/Chinese Taipei in AFC women's brackets and do not conflate "China" with "Chinese Taipei".
- Liverpool first-leg only goal scorer resolved to Mario Lemina. I missed by anchoring on Liverpool attackers. Lesson: "before second leg" questions can ask about the opponent's first-leg scorer; include defensive/midfield opponent scorers.

Updates made:
- IPL opener: moved RCB's opening opponent to Punjab Kings as the stronger defending-champion versus previous finalist prior, while keeping CSK and Mumbai as marquee-opponent tails.
- PSG-Chelsea substitute scorer: no exact March 9 result yet; broadened from Ramos/Barcola to include Desire Doue, No player, and Lee Kang-in.
- Medical-device cyberattack claimant: March 9 JPost reported Iran-linked data-wiping attacks across Israeli organisations. Shifted from anti-Iran Predatory Sparrow toward Iran-linked/public hacktivist personas Handala and CyberAv3ngers while keeping Predatory Sparrow as a tail.
- Real Madrid-Man City hat-trick scorer: no exact article; kept Jude Bellingham first, added Vinicius Junior and Haaland while lowering Rodrygo because he has prior hat-trick history.
- Central Beirut organisation: March 9 reports described the target as commanders of the "Lebanon Corps" of Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force. Added Lebanon Corps as an exact-label hedge behind Quds Force/IRGC.
- AFC South Korea-final opponent: China topped Group B, North Korea finished second and will face Australia in a quarterfinal. Lowered Australia and raised North Korea for the team that could beat South Korea to the final.
- AFC World Cup playoff route: because North Korea vs Australia is the March 13 quarterfinal, shifted the playoff-survivor forecast to North Korea/Australia instead of China/South Korea.
- Western Iraq aircraft model: NDTV/Al Jazeera evidence points to a lost or failed LUCAS one-way attack drone in western Iraq. Raised LUCAS and exact-system variants.
- Trump/Iranian island: Qeshm remains repeatedly linked to US strikes and desalination/military-installation reporting. Raised Qeshm Island/Qeshm.
- Detroit synagogue and Virginia university: no exact reporting, but broadened narrow priors to include major Detroit-area synagogues and Virginia Tech/VCU tails.
- Gulf water/power plant country: Bahrain reported refinery and desalination damage from Iranian attacks; raised Bahrain further for the utility-site worker-death country.

Watched but no update:
- Michigan synagogue assailant, KC-135 base country, US troop ship, southern Lebanon health-centre village, launch-area country, Spain friendly opponent, Hormuz nonparticipation country, news-network obituary, Formula One Chinese GP DNS constructor, Mediterranean rescue NGO, Ukraine missile type, Chris Murphy/Greg Casar bill name, and Abu Dhabi company-site damage had no March 9 exact-answer signal strong enough for a sharper move.

## 2026-03-10 Update

Resolution events:
- RCB IPL opener resolved to Sunrisers Hyderabad. I missed by over-weighting the prior-year finalist Punjab Kings and marquee CSK/MI options. Lesson: include Sunrisers Hyderabad in IPL-opener markets, especially where schedule makers want a high-run/rematch-style opener.
- PSG substitute two-goal scorer resolved to Khvicha Kvaratskhelia. I missed by assuming he would start and focusing on central-forward substitutes. Lesson: in PSG scorer markets, include Kvaratskhelia even when the question says substitute.
- Medical-device cyberattack claimant resolved to Handala. The March 9 shift toward Iran-linked personas worked. Lesson: for retaliatory cyber claims tied to Israel/Iran, Handala deserves material first-line probability.
- Real Madrid-Man City first-career hat-trick resolved to Federico Valverde. I missed by focusing on attackers. Lesson: first-career hat-trick markets can be midfielder surprises; include Valverde/Camavinga-type long-shot exact names in Real Madrid markets.
- Central Beirut organisation resolved to Jama'a Islamiye. I missed by taking Israeli military Quds Force language too literally. Lesson: Beirut apartment/Israeli media allegation markets can resolve to Lebanese Sunni/Palestinian-linked groups such as Jama'a Islamiye, not only Hezbollah/IRGC/Quds Force.

Updates made:
- Gulf water/power plant country: March 10 Irish Times named the UAE's Fujairah water and power complex and Kuwait's Doha West desalination plant as damaged by intercepted Iranian drones/missiles. Shifted lead from Bahrain to UAE/Kuwait because those sites better match "electricity and fresh water".
- KC-135 base country: reduced Saudi overconfidence. Prince Sultan remains the strongest missile-target clue, but fresh KC-135-specific satellite reporting places a KC-135 at Al Udeid in Qatar, and Spain/Moron/Rota KC-135 movements remain a small tail.
- Launch-area country: added Iraq because Al Jazeera reported Iraq's PM telling Rubio that Iraq's airspace, territory, and waters should not be used for attacks on neighbouring countries. UAE remains first because of the direct al-Dhafra launchpad allegation.
- Hormuz nonparticipation country: raised Spain further after renewed reporting on Spain's "No to war" position and U.S. anger over Rota/Moron, while France, Britain, Germany, Italy, and others move toward shipping-support deployments.
- Western Iraq aircraft model: kept LUCAS first but restored an F-15E Strike Eagle tail because CENTCOM's official aircraft-loss reporting around the same operation names F-15E, even though that loss was over Kuwait rather than western Iraq.

Watched but no update:
- Michigan synagogue name and assailant, Virginia university shooting, Trump/Qeshm island, US troop ship, southern Lebanon health-centre village, AFC Women's playoff path, Spain friendly opponent, news-network obituary, Chinese GP DNS constructor, Mediterranean rescue NGO, South Korea AFC semifinal opponent, Ukraine missile type, Israeli official quote, and Chris Murphy/Greg Casar bill name had no March 10 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-03-11 Update

Resolution events:
- Michigan synagogue assailant resolved to Ayman Mohamad Ghazali. I missed, but kept probabilities tiny so the damage was limited. Lesson: if there is no direct reporting, keep suspect-name forecasts low rather than inventing confidence.
- Detroit-area synagogue resolved to Temple Israel. I had Temple Israel in the set at 0.16 but not first. Lesson: Temple Israel is a core Detroit-area synagogue prior for active-shooter/major incident markets.
- Western Iraq aircraft model resolved to KC-135. I missed badly by focusing on LUCAS/F-15E. Lesson: when adjacent market wording mentions KC-135 damage, include KC-135 as an aircraft-model answer even if "aircraft model" feels odd.
- Virginia university resolved to Old Dominion University. I missed by omitting ODU. Lesson: Virginia campus-shooting markets need Old Dominion University alongside George Mason, UVA, Virginia Tech, VCU, and Liberty.

Updates made:
- KC-135 base country: the KC-135 aircraft-model resolution plus March 11 reporting of strikes on Al Harir in Iraq moved Iraq to first, ahead of Qatar/Saudi/Kuwait/UAE.
- Trump/Iranian island: Kharg Island became newly salient after Straits Times reported it was in the crosshairs and that U.S. officials had discussed capturing it. Shifted Kharg Island narrowly above Qeshm Island.
- Spain friendly opponent: Straits Times explicitly said the Spain-Argentina clash is set for Doha on March 27. Raised Argentina sharply.
- Launch-area country: repeated Iraq launchpad/airspace warnings and attacks on Iraq's Kurdistan region raised Iraq, though UAE remains first because of al-Dhafra.
- Israeli official quote: Israel Katz continues to be the Israeli defence official quoted on Iran operation objectives and successors. Raised Katz.
- US troop ship: no exact hit; broadened from Bataan/Wasp to include Iwo Jima, Kearsarge, and America.
- Southern Lebanon health-centre village: no exact hit; broadened from Mays al-Jabal/Taybeh to include Maifadoun, Ain Baal, and Kafr Kila.
- Mediterranean rescue NGO, Chinese GP DNS constructor, and news-network obituary: no exact hits, but broadened priors to include Sea-Eye/Open Arms/MSF, Williams/Sauber/Racing Bulls, and Sky/BBC/Fox respectively.

Watched but no update:
- AFC Women's playoff path, South Korea AFC semifinal opponent, Hormuz nonparticipation country, Ukraine missile type, Arsenal quarterfinal opponent, Mexico refinery, Chris Murphy/Greg Casar bill name, and Gulf utility-site country had no March 11 exact-answer signal strong enough for a sharper move.

London favourite to host Spain v Argentina Finalissima after Doha doubts

MADRID, March 3 - Soccer chiefs from Europe and South America will hold a final meeting before a Thursday deadline to decide whether and where this month's "Finalissima" between Spain and Argentina will be played, with London emerging as the leading candidate after doubts over Doha, multiple sources told Reuters on Tuesday. The match between European champions Spain and Copa America holders Argentina had been scheduled for March 27 at Lusail Stadium in Doha. However, it has become increasingly unlikely that Qatar will host the fixture after the Qatar Football Association suspended soccer tournaments indefinitely following U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and retaliatory missiles fired at the Arabian Peninsula. The Spanish FA (RFEF) has been pushing for a swift resolution, mindful that the March international break is viewed as vital preparation ahead of the June-July World Cup in North America. "I know that negotiations are underway,” Spain coach Luis de la Fuente told Spanish Public Radio (RNE) on Monday. “The first thing, as a society, is to stop the conflict, but once you are immersed in it and you don't know how long it will last, the solution would be, as long as you can't play there, to find another venue as soon as possible."
Wembley Stadium staged the previous edition in 2022, when Argentina beat Italy, but it is set to host England v Uruguay on March 27. London, however, has other stadiums capable of staging the showpiece, leaving the English capital as the most likely alternative should Doha be ruled out, sources confirmed. ALTERNATIVE OPPONENTS CONSIDERED
While keen to face Argentina and high-profile players such as Lionel Messi, sources told Reuters that Spain had made clear their priority was not to waste the last window of international fixtures before the World Cup and they were already contemplating alternative opponents.

Historical Baggage
A longstanding territorial dispute continues to cast a shadow over UAE-Iran relations. The islands of Abu Musa, Greater Tunb and Lesser Tunb, located near the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz, were occupied by Iran in 1971 shortly before the UAE's formation and remain under Iranian control. The issue remains an enduring irritant in bilateral ties. Iranian attacks on the UAE are not unprecedented. In January 2022, Iran-backed Houthi forces launched "Operation Hurricane Yemen", targeting oil infrastructure and the Abu Dhabi International Airport. Three civilians were killed, including two Indian nationals. The damage, however, was contained. Following the Saudi-Iran rapprochement in 2023 mediated by China, UAE-Iran relations experienced a diplomatic thaw. Full diplomatic relations were restored, security consultations resumed, and economic delegations were exchanged. Economic engagement and de-escalation appeared to define the trajectory - until now.

Drone attack targets UAE’s Musaffah fuel tank terminal, no impact on operations

CAIRO, March 2 - A fire broke out after Musaffah fuel tank terminal in the United Arab Emirates was targeted by a drone, without impacting its operations, Abu Dhbai media office said on Monday. "Abu Dhabi authorities have responded today to a fire resulting from the targeting of a Musaffah fuel tank terminal by a drone. The situation was promptly contained," the office said. No injuries were reported. Abu Dhabi National Oil Company operates a facility in Musaffah from which fuel is transported by trucks, while a 1,600-kilometre pipeline network distributes finished products across the emirate of Abu Dhabi. Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis targeted the Mussafah Fuel Depot in 2022 during the group's war with the Saudi-led coalition. Earlier on Monday, Saudi Arabia's state oil giant Aramco shut its Ras Tanura refinery after it was hit by a drone in an apparent escalation on the third day of strikes across the region launched by Tehran in response to the U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran. QatarEnergy also said it was halting production of LNG and associated products due to attacks on facilities in Ras Laffan, a source with knowledge told Reuters. REUTERS

Saudi Aramco Suspends Operations at Ras Tanura Refinery After Reported Drone Strike

Saudi Aramco has temporarily halted operations at its Ras Tanura refinery following reports of a drone strike in the area, Bloomberg reported, citing sources familiar with the situation. The company’s media office did not immediately respond to requests for comment. According to Turkiye Today, an Iranian drone allegedly targeted the facility early Monday, triggering a fire at the site. Thick plumes of smoke were reportedly seen rising from the refinery, with visuals circulating online showing the aftermath of the incident. The outlet, however, described the reports as “unconfirmed” at the time. Reuters later reported that the fire, described as “small” and isolated, was brought under control following the suspected drone strike. Ras Tanura is Saudi Aramco’s oldest and most advanced refinery, with the ability to process up to 550,000 barrels of crude oil and condensates daily. The facility is closely linked to the Ras Tanura Port, the world’s largest crude oil export terminal, which serves as a vital hub for shipments from Saudi Arabia to Asia, Europe, and the United States. Officials are continuing to monitor the situation, but there have been no reports of casualties. The incident comes amid rising tensions in the region and highlights the vulnerability of key energy infrastructure to regional conflicts.
